title: Listening - Part 4: Starting a Repair Café
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a woman called Marina, who runs a community repair café. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: This evening I’m joined by Marina Cole, who started a repair café in her town. Marina, what exactly is a repair café? || Marina: It’s a regular event where people bring broken household items and volunteers help repair them. We have tools, tea and plenty of patience. [24]The idea started when neighbours kept bringing me lamps and kettles after they heard I could fix small electrical things[/24]. I realised people didn’t want to throw useful objects away, but didn’t know where to begin. || Presenter: So it isn’t an ordinary repair shop? || Marina: No. We never promise that everything will work again, and we don’t simply take an object away from its owner. [25]We encourage visitors to sit with the volunteer and take part in the repair themselves[/25]. Next time a loose wire or missing button appears, they may feel confident enough to deal with it. || Presenter: What was hardest when you set it up? || Marina: Finding a room was easy; the library offered us a space. I had lots of tools already. [26]The difficult part was persuading skilled people that volunteering one Saturday a month would be enjoyable rather than an extra responsibility[/26]. Once two retired engineers joined, others followed. || Presenter: What sorts of things do people bring? || Marina: We expected radios, lamps and toasters. Those certainly come in, but our busiest table now involves needles and thread. [27]The clothing repair sessions have attracted far more visitors than our electrical repairs[/27], partly because young people want to change old clothes rather than buy new ones. || Presenter: How do you know whether the café is successful? || Marina: It’s nice when we receive donations, because we need materials, but money isn’t the aim. [28]For me, success is seeing an object leave in someone’s bag ready to be used again instead of going into a rubbish bin[/28]. Even a repaired zip can keep a favourite coat in use for years. || Presenter: Has running the café changed your view of modern products? || Marina: It has. Some manufacturers are helpful and supply replacement parts. Unfortunately, [29]many newer appliances are difficult to open safely, so a tiny fault can make the whole object impossible for us to repair[/29]. That can be frustrating when the older version of the same product was simple to fix. || Presenter: What would you say to someone wanting to create a similar project? || Marina: Don’t wait until you can afford a special building or a complete set of tools. [30]Begin with a small, regular event in a borrowed room and learn what your community actually needs[/30]. You may discover that one person with sewing skills is more useful than ten expensive machines. || Presenter: Marina, thank you for coming in. || Marina: Thank you. I hope listeners check whether something can be repaired before replacing it.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: Why did Marina first consider starting a repair café?

options.A: People nearby regularly asked her for help with repairs.
options.B: A local library requested a practical activity.
options.C: She wanted to sell tools she no longer used.

questions.explanation: Marina says neighbours repeatedly brought broken lamps and kettles to her for help.

questions.question: How is Marina's repair café different from a normal repair shop?

options.A: It only accepts objects that are easy to repair.
options.B: Visitors are involved in repairing their own objects.
options.C: It provides replacement products while repairs are done.

questions.explanation: Marina encourages visitors to work alongside volunteers during repairs.

questions.question: What did Marina initially find most challenging?

options.A: Obtaining enough tools for the café.
options.B: Finding a suitable place to hold events.
options.C: Convincing skilled volunteers to give their time.

questions.explanation: She found it hard to persuade experienced people that volunteering would be enjoyable.

questions.question: What has surprised Marina about the visitors?

options.A: Their greatest interest is in repairing clothes.
options.B: Most of them bring electrical equipment.
options.C: They are mainly older people with practical skills.

questions.explanation: She says the clothing repair sessions are much busier than electrical repairs.

questions.question: What is Marina's main measure of success?

options.A: Receiving enough donations to buy materials.
options.B: Preventing usable objects from being thrown away.
options.C: Teaching visitors to repair every object independently.

questions.explanation: For Marina, success means an object is used again rather than placed in the rubbish.

questions.question: What criticism does Marina make of some modern appliances?

options.A: They cannot always be opened easily for repair.
options.B: Their replacement parts are too expensive to order.
options.C: They break down more frequently than older products.

questions.explanation: She says many newer appliances are difficult to open safely, making minor faults hard to repair.

questions.question: What advice does Marina give about starting a repair café?

options.A: Invest in high-quality equipment from the beginning.
options.B: Concentrate on electrical repairs before other skills.
options.C: Start modestly and respond to local demand.

questions.explanation: She recommends beginning with a small regular event and finding out what the community needs.
